Alex Ross' article "The Trill of Doom" described this Hungarian-born pianist's struggle to interpret the trill in Schubert's sonata. In 2015 and 2016 he gave a series of "Last Sonatas" recitals highlighting the final piano sonatas of Mozart, Haydn, Beethoven, and Schubert.

bonuses.leadin: The final movement of a piece with this nickname is a tarantella in D minor, and its second movement features variations on a song its composer had written earlier. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this nickname of a piece that followed its composer’s previous entry in the same genre, titled Rosamunde.
Death and the Maiden is a string quartet by this composer of the Trout Quintet and the Unfinished Symphony.
This final chamber work of Schubert contains his most famous adagio in its second movement. It was partially inspired by Mozart’s work in the same genre and key.
answers: Death and the Maiden [or Der Tod und das Mädchen]
answers_sanitized: Death and the Maiden [or Der Tod und das Madchen]

bonuses.leadin: This piano sonata’s rondo finale begins in C minor with a long G octave in the left hand, then, like the finale of Beethoven’s String Quartet No. 13, cheerfully modulates down a step. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this piano sonata written months before Schubert’s early death in 1828. A mysterious G-flat trill in the bass intrudes eight measures into this sonata, and its C-sharp minor slow movement unusually ends in major.

Schubert wrote 8 pieces in this free-form genre invented by Jan Voříšek; the first one opens with held G octaves in both hands. A Chopin piece in this genre, prefixed “Fantaisie,” opens with a long G-sharp octave in the left hand.

Fantaisie-Impromptu launches into a 4-against-3 cross-rhythm, an example of the “vertical” type of this device. This syncopation-like device of juxtaposing duple and triple rhythm appears in many Baroque cadences, Schubert impromptus, and works by Brahms.
answers: Piano Sonata in B-flat major by Franz Schubert [or D. 960; accept Piano Sonata No. 21 (or No. 23) by Franz Schubert; accept descriptions like the final piano sonata by Franz Schubert]
